coredata.dc:title: Waterborne epoxy composite coating containing N,S-doped carbon dots for enhancing corrosion resistance
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haojie
Deng, Shuo
Liu, Xiaohui
Liu, Hua
Gao, Runying
Zeng, Birong
Xu, Yiting
Chen, Guorong
Luo, Weiang
Yuan, Conghui
Dai, Lizong
coredata.dc:description: In order to construct waterborne epoxy composite coating with enhanced anti-corrosive performance, the development of efficient additives with good compatibility with adhesive resin and good interaction with steel matrix is of significance. Herein, we adapted two heteroatom (N and S) doped carbon quantum dots, namely ACDs and LCDs, as eco-friendly anti-corrosion components to mix with water-based epoxy resin (WP) to prepare the CDs/WP composite coating. Compared with the ACDs/WP composite, the LCDs/WP composite exhibited a better performance because it contained more functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl on the surface of LCDs, which not only made the system denser but also facilitated the multi-anchor coordination and adsorption on carbon steel. Following immersing in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 28 d, the impedance modulus of LCDs/WP surpassed 109 Ω cm2, exhibiting approximately three orders of magnitude higher values compared to pure WP coatings. The inhibition efficiency was improved by 96.9%. The facile synthesis of LCDs coupled with excellent anti-corrosive properties of LCDs/WP composites, provides a new way for developing anti-corrosion coating materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Waterborne epoxy resin
Carbon steel
Anti-corrosion
Surface

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image for (a) ACDs and (b) LCDs. (b) FTIR spectra; (d) XRD pattern.
Equivalent circuit models of stage I (a) and stage II (b); (c) coating resistance (log Rc); (d) charge transfer resistance (log Rct).
Visual observations exposing to salt spray test environment before and after 35d, (a) WP; (b) 0.5ACDs/WP; (c) 0.5LCDs/WP and (d) 1.0LCDs/WP coating.

SEM and EDS spectra of corrosion region on steel surface under (a) WP; (b) 0.5ACDs/WP; (c) 0.5LCDs/WP; (d) 1.0LCDs/WP coating. (e–h) XPS spectra of corrosion product under 0.5AL-CQDsWEP coating immersing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 28 d: (e) full spectrum; (f) N 1 s; (g) S 2p; (h) Fe 2p.
Corrosion protective mechanism diagram of all coatings: (a) WP; (b) 0.5ACDs/WP; (c) 0.5LCDs/WP; (d) 1.0LCDs/WP.

head.ce:title: Waterborne epoxy composite coating containing N,S-doped carbon dots for enhancing corrosion resistance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials
Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to construct waterborne epoxy composite coating with enhanced anti-corrosive performance, the development of efficient additives with good compatibility with adhesive resin and good interaction with steel matrix is of significance. Herein, we adapted two heteroatom (N and S) doped carbon quantum dots, namely ACDs and LCDs, as eco-friendly anti-corrosion components to mix with water-based epoxy resin (WP) to prepare the CDs/WP composite coating. Compared with the ACDs/WP composite, the LCDs/WP composite exhibited a better performance because it contained more functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl on the surface of LCDs, which not only made the system denser but also facilitated the multi-anchor coordination and adsorption on carbon steel. Following immersing in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 28 d, the impedance modulus of LCDs/WP surpassed 109 Ω cm2, exhibiting approximately three orders of magnitude higher values compared to pure WP coatings. The inhibition efficiency was improved by 96.9%. The facile synthesis of LCDs coupled with excellent anti-corrosive properties of LCDs/WP composites, provides a new way for developing anti-corrosion coating materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Low carbon steel is widely used in the fields of construction, automotive, mechanical manufacturing, and household appliance industries. It offers significant advantages in terms of yield strength, high tensile strength, and ductility in its applications [1,2]. However, corrosion on steel can severely compromise its excellent mechanical properties, leading to significant financial losses and even posing a threat to human safety [3,4]. Therefore, necessary protective measures must be taken to enhance the corrosion resistance of metals.

Epoxy resin is considered one of the most effective resin materials for anticorrosion protection of metal. Among them, waterborne epoxy resin has attracted much attention because of their advantages such as strong adaptability, environmental friendliness, true water-based nature, and excellent workability [5,6]. At present it is imperative to create composite coatings that have both barrier properties and corrosion inhibition capabilities.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are a type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial [7,8]. Recently, heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots, known for their outstanding water solubility, chemical stability, cost-effectiveness, and non-toxic properties, have emerged as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors for inhibiting steel corrosion [9]. However, CDs are often formulated as corrosion inhibitor solution to inhibit the corrosion of steel plates, but are rarely utilized as additive components in coatings. Therefore, the investigation of CDs-based polymer composite coatings is of significance and popular.

Herein, two types of N,S-doped CDs, ACDs and LCDs, by using 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole-5-thiol (ATT) as a carbon source individually and in combination with l-tyrosine (LT), were prepared (Fig. S1) and then were incorporated into waterborne epoxy resin to produce CDs/WP composite coatings. The chemical properties of CDs and the thermal property as well as corrosion resistance performances of CDs/WP coating on carbon steel were explored. Finally, the mechanism of LCDs in inhibiting the corrosion of carbon steel was proposed.

TEM characterization in Fig. 1 a,b showed the synthesized CDs had a lattice fringes spacing of 0.21 nm, aligned with the interplanar spacing of the graphene (001) plane [10]. The average particle sizes of ACDs and LCDs were about 2.47 nm (Fig. S2a) and 1.81 nm (Fig. S2b) respectively. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 1c) showed that ACDs not only possessed some similar functional groups as ATT compound, but also had a unique functional group at 2063 cm−1 representing the stretching vibration of –NCS-, which was generated from carbonization reactions [11]. The characteristic absorption peaks of LCDs appeared at around 1659 (amide I: related to CO stretching vibration), 1512 (amide II: in-plane N–H bending vibration), and 1326 cm−1 (amide III: related to C–N in amide bond vibration), confirming the existence of amide groups in LCDs [12]. Moreover, the surface of LCDs was rich in oxygen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl O–H (observed at 3400 cm−1) and C–O- (observed at 1230 cm−1). The UV–vis spectra (Fig. S2c) showed that only one absorption peak at 227 nm was observed for ACDs while three peaks at 220 nm, 246 nm, and 330 nm were observed for LCDs. Both 246 nm and 330 nm were assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC [13]. Obviously, the n-π* transition of CN was blue-shifted from 227 nm to 220 nm, indicating an interaction between the introduced LT and ATT [14]. The XRD spectra (Fig. 1d) showed both the two CDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak in the range of 2θ = 15°–25°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [15]. In the XPS spectra of LCDs (Figs. S2d–h), peaks corresponding to C, N, O, and S were all observed. The above results indicated that ACDs and LCDs were successfully synthesized.

EIS experiments were carried out to assess the corrosion resistance of the coatings. Both Bode and Nyquist plots (Fig. 2 ) depicted the coatings immersing in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 28 d. As depicted in Figs. S5a and a substantial decline in the impedance modulus (Z0.01) of WP was evident, which was attributed to the rapid deterioration of the WP coating. However, the Z0.01 of 0.5LCDs/WP reached 7.16 × 109 Ω cm2 in the initial stages of immersion, which was approximately two times of magnitude higher than that of WP, and about one time of magnitude higher than that of 0.5ACDs/WP. Nevertheless, during the initial stages of immersion, the Z0.01 value for the 1.0LCDs/WP coating was comparably elevated. However, as the immersion time increased, the Z0.01 value of the 1.0LCDs/WP coating experienced a swift decline. After 28 days of immersion, the Z0.01 value was 1.51 × 108 Ω cm2, which was lower than that of the 0.5ACDs/WP (2.97 × 108 Ω cm2). Conversely, the Z0.01 value of the 0.5LCDs/WP coating sustained a higher level even after 28 days of immersion. It was concluded that the 0.5LCDs/WP coating showcased superior barrier properties against the steel substrate. The Bode phase angle plots (Fig. S5b) also proved that the 0.5LCDs/WP coating had the best corrosion resistance among these CDs/WP composites.

The R(QR(QR)) and R(QR(Q(RZw))) models were employed in Fig. 3 a, b to fit the equivalent circuit models [17,18]. The inclusion of the Warburg element (Zw) was utilized to depict the diffusion occurring within the coating [19,20]. Fig. 3c, d illustrated the coating resistance (Rc) and double-layer charge transfer resistance (Rct) for distinct coating systems following a 28-day immersion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The WP coating exhibited a gradual reduction in both Rc and Rct values over the test duration. In contrast, after the addition of ACDs and LCDs, the Rc and Rct values of the CDs/WP remained consistently high, although they slightly decreased over the entire immersion period. The ranking of Rc and Rct values from highest to lowest was as follows: 0.5LCDs/WP, 0.5ACDs/WP ≈ 1.0LCDs/WP. To further confirm the effectiveness of CDs in inhibiting corrosion, the potentiodynamic polarization curves and corrosion parameters were tested in Fig. S6 and Table S1. The results proved that adequate encapsulation of CDs could reduce coating defects and increase the diffusion resistance of corrosive substances within the coating matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Salt spray tests and laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM)
ce:section.ce:para: A salt spray test (Fig. 4 ) was conducted to delve deeper validated the corrosion resistance of the coatings. After 35 days of testing, the steel plate with the WP coating exhibited extensive rusting, and numerous corrosion spots appeared on the surface of the WP coating (Fig. 4a2). Conversely, the condition of corrosion on the steel plate, shielded by the 0.5LCDs/WP coating, exhibited an improvement (Fig. 4b2). We also used LSCM to analyze the steel surfaces after corrosion in NaCl solution under different coating protections. As shown in Fig. S7, the surface roughness of the steel protected by 0.5LCDs/WP was the lowest (0.945 μm), confirmed that a minimal damage occurred. It was inferred that LCDs could interact with the epoxy matrix throughout active groups such as –OH, –SH and –NH. And the gap-filling effect of LCDs within WP rendered it more challenging for corrosive substances to penetrate the surface of the steel plate.

Fig. 5 displayed SEM images and EDS spectra depicting the corroded regions on the steel surface beneath coatings. The steel surface protected by the WP coating was severely damaged by corrosive particles, forming a dense network and porous structure (Fig. 5a1). The Fe, O, and Cl elements on the steel surface indicated a severe corrosion (Fig. 5a2). Among all the coatings, the steel surface protected by the 0.5LCDs/WP coating, while developing cracks after corrosion, exhibited the least corrosion compared to other coatings (Fig. 5c1). The Cl element content was the lowest (Fig. 5c2), confirming that the introduction of –OH, –COOH, –SH and -NH2 could enhance the corrosion resistance of the WP. Fig. 5c showed the XPS spectra of corrosion products on the steel plate protected by the 0.5LCDs/WP coating immersing in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 28 d. We found the peak at 398.7 eV (corresponding to N–Fe) in the N 1s spectrum and the peak at 156.4 eV (corresponding to Fe–S) in the S 2p spectrum [21]. These findings robustly affirmed the chemical adsorption of LCDs. In the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 5h), the two peaks could be delineated into peaks at 708.8 and 722.4 eV for Fe2+, 710.6 and 725.0 eV for Fe3+ and a satellite peak of γ-Fe2O3 was evident at 716.9 eV [22]. The outcomes suggested that the predominant constituents of the protective film were γ-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4, validating that the protective film induced by LCDs in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution environment primarily constitutes a passive film.

As illustrated in Fig. 6 , the incorporation of CDs served multiple functions in providing corrosion resistance. Initially, serving as a nanofiller, incorporating a suitable quantity of CDs into the water-based epoxy resin coating structure could effectively fill voids and address defects resulting from water evaporation during the curing process. Secondly, the interactions between CDs and epoxy matrix, such as hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, and covalent bonds, could make the water-based epoxy resin network denser, thereby enhancing its corrosion resistance. Finally, the chemical adsorption and coordination interactions of organic corrosion inhibitors with the steel surface were essential corrosion resistance mechanisms. This chemical adsorption occurred because heteroatoms like N, S, O, with lone pairs of electrons, could easily fill the empty 3d orbitals of Fe atoms, forming coordination covalent bonds between the heteroatoms and the steel surface. As for ACDs, it contained amino and triazole rings that could serve as adsorption centers to inhibit the corrosion of the steel plate. As for LCDs, the more active groups not only provided LCDs with electrostatic attraction to the metal surface but can also acted as multiple adsorption centers, leading to the formation of a denser and thicker protective film. As a result, the corrosion resistance of the 0.5LCDs/WP coating was significantly improved (Fig. 6b).

Two types of CDs, namely ACDs and LCDs were synthesized. Structural characterization indicated that LCDs contained more abundant functional groups including hydroxyl, amino, sulfhydryl, unsaturated bonds, and heterocycles than ACDs. EIS results demonstrated that the composite coating of 0.5LCDs/WP displayed superior corrosion resistance for Q235 steel. Tafel results demonstrated that the corrosion inhibition rate for steel plates protected by a low addition of 0.5LCDs/WP coatings reached 96.9%. SEM and XPS confirmed the adsorption of LCDs onto the steel surface. This led to the formation of a stable protective layer, effectively preventing interaction between the steel plate and corrosive elements. Based on these findings, the introduction of carbon quantum dots not only could increase the crosslinking density of waterborne epoxy resin, but also had the ability to adhere to the steel plate's surface through metal coordination, creating a protective film. Furthermore, the nanosized CDs could fill the coating defect. In summary, the conception of incorporating N,S-doped CDs into waterborne epoxy would expand their applications in the field of anticorrosion protection.

Haojie Wang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Shuo Deng: Formal analysis. Xiaohui Liu: Methodology. Hua Liu: Investigation. Runying Gao: Project administration. Birong Zeng: Writing – review & editing. Yiting Xu: Project administration. Guorong Chen: Resources. Weiang Luo: Software. Conghui Yuan: Supervision. Lizong Dai: Visualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52173081, 52273275), Central Government Guidance Local Science and Technology Development Funds in Fujian Province (2022L3002).
